prompt:
Perf ticket from the platform-pricing crew. stackcost.py rolls up the price of a deployment stack: a catalog maps stack templates to unit_cents plus (child, count) parts, and cost = unit_cents + count * cost(child) over the parts, exact ints, with a part reached along two paths paid once per path. It was written against pilot catalogs maybe ten templates deep and it was fine. The new platform catalog composes base modules everywhere — lots of diamond shapes — and the quote endpoint now just never comes back on real stacks; for anything nontrivial the walk blows up combinatorially with depth, because every shared part gets re-walked once per path even though its cost obviously never changes within one estimate() call.

Fix the performance without changing a single answer. Semantics stay exactly as the module docstring pins them: path-counting cost (values must be identical, they're exact ints so no wiggle room), UnknownStack / CycleError with the offending stack named in the message, and the on_expand hook still fires once each time a stack's cost is actually computed — but handing back an already-computed result must not fire it. The perf suite budgets expansions through that hook at 500k for a 723-stack ladder that has ~2^240 paths, and it also puts a generous tracemalloc ceiling on peak memory, so don't trade the CPU blowup for a memory one.

python3 test_stackcost.py is the acceptance contract; leave the test file alone.
